items.foco: Proximal ulna fracture with radial head dislocation
items.title: Definition and key concept
items.contentHtml:
Fracture of the proximal ulna with dislocation of the radial head

  • Monteggia injury is defined as fracture of the proximal ulna (proximal third or diaphysis) associated with dislocation of the head of the radius; The dislocation can be anterior, posterior or lateral depending on the type.
  • Classic mechanism: fall with forearm in forced pronation or direct blow to the posterior surface of the ulna; in children it is usually due to hyperextension.
  • The diagnosis requires an x-ray of the forearm that includes the elbow and wrist; the radiocapitellar line must pass through the center of the humeral condyle in all projections; If not, suspect dislocation.
  • Trap: in children, the radial head does not ossify until 4-5 years of age, so the radiocapitellar line over the center of the humeral condyle is evaluated.

items.foco: Bado classification: types I, II, III and IV
items.title: Bado Rating
items.contentHtml:
Bado classification: types I, II, III and IV

  • Proposed by Bado in 1967, it divides the Monteggia lesion into four types according to the direction of the radial dislocation and location of the ulnar fracture.
  • Type I: anterior dislocation of the radial head + diaphyseal fracture of the ulna with anterior angulation; It is the most frequent (≈60%), typical in children.
  • Type II: Posterior or posterolateral dislocation of the radial head + metaphyseal fracture of the ulna with posterior angulation; predominates in adults (≈15%).
  • Type III: lateral dislocation of the radial head + fracture of the proximal ulna (metaphysis); almost exclusively in children (≈20%).
  • Type IV: anterior dislocation of the radial head + fracture of both bones of the forearm (ulna and radius at the diaphyseal level); the rarest (≈5%).

items.title: Type I: extension in children
items.contentHtml:
Type I: extension (children, diaphyseal fracture)

  • Monteggia injury type I: anterior dislocation of the radial head with diaphyseal fracture of the ulna with anterior angulation (towards flexion).
  • Typical mechanism: fall with forearm in pronation and elbow in forced extension; the ulna undergoes an axial compressive force.
  • In children, the treatment of choice is closed reduction under anesthesia, followed by immobilization with a brachiopalmar cast in supination and elbow flexion >90° for 4-6 weeks.
  • Trap: if closed reduction fails or there is instability, open reduction and plate fixation are required; The radial head should reduce spontaneously as the length of the ulna is restored.

items.title: Type II: flexion in adults
items.contentHtml:
Type II: flexion (adults, metaphyseal fracture)

  • Monteggia injury type II: posterior or posterolateral dislocation of the radial head with metaphyseal fracture of the ulna (proximal third) and posterior angulation.
  • It is the most common variant in adults; mechanism: fall with forearm in supination and elbow in flexion.
  • Treatment is surgical in adults: fixation with plate and ulna screws to restore length and alignment; the radial head reduces spontaneously after fixation.
  • Trap: in type II, the radial dislocation can be posterolateral, and is often associated with fracture of the humeral condyle or the coronoid process; Always evaluate the elbow joint.

items.foco: Type III: proximal ulna fracture in children
items.title: Type III: proximal ulna fracture in children
items.contentHtml:
Type III: fracture of the proximal ulna in children

  • Monteggia injury type III: lateral dislocation of the radial head with fracture of the proximal ulna (metaphysis), almost exclusive to the pediatric population.
  • Mechanism: fall with forearm in pronation and elbow in extension, generating forced varus of the elbow.
  • In children, the initial treatment is closed reduction with manipulation and casting; If the dislocation persists, open reduction is indicated with or without fixation of the ulna.
  • Trap: in type III, the radial head is dislocated laterally, and can be confused with a fracture of the lateral humeral condyle; The radiocapitellar line is key to differentiate.

items.foco: Type IV: fracture of both bones with radial dislocation
items.title: Type IV: fracture of both bones with radial dislocation
items.contentHtml:
Type IV: fracture of both bones with radial dislocation

  • Monteggia injury type IV: anterior dislocation of the radial head with diaphyseal fracture of both the ulna and radius (both bones of the forearm).
  • It is the least common variant (≈5%) and the most serious; requires mandatory surgical management at all ages.
  • Treatment: open reduction and internal fixation of both bones with plates and screws; the radial head is reduced after restoring the length of the ulna and radius.
  • Trap: not to be confused with Galeazzi fracture (distal radius fracture with dislocation of the distal radioulnar joint); In Monteggia type IV the dislocation is proximal (radial head) and the radius fracture is diaphyseal.

items.title: Posterior interosseous nerve injury
items.contentHtml:
Injury of the posterior interosseous nerve (most common)

  • The most common neurological complication in Monteggia injury is injury to the posterior interosseous nerve (motor branch of the radial nerve), due to its proximity to the dislocated radial head.
  • Clinically it produces deficits in extension of the fingers (mainly metacarpophalangeal) and thumb, without loss sensitive on the back of the hand.
  • The injury is usually due to stretching or compression; most recover spontaneously after reduction of the radial head in 3-6 months.
  • Entrapment: if the deficit persists >6 months or there is evidence of entrapment (for example, in fracture-dislocation type II), it may require surgical exploration and neurolysis.

items.foco: Closed reduction in children, surgical reduction in adults
items.title: Management according to age
items.contentHtml:
Closed reduction in children, surgical in adults

  • In children (immature skeletons), the first-line treatment for most Monteggia lesions is closed reduction under general anesthesia, followed by immobilization with a brachiopalmar cast in supination and elbow flexion >90° for 4-6 weeks.
  • In adults, management is surgical: fixation with plate and ulna screws to restore length and alignment; the radial head is reduced spontaneously or openly reduced if necessary.
  • Indications for surgery in children: failure of closed reduction, instability, comminuted fracture or type IV injury; In these cases, open reduction and fixation with plate or nails are performed.
  • Trap: in children, successful closed reduction requires that the radial head be stable; If it dislocates again when mobilizing the elbow, surgery must be performed.

items.foco: Fixation with plate and screws in adult ulna
items.title: Plate fixation in adults
items.contentHtml:
Adult ulna plate and screw fixation

  • In adults with Monteggia lesion, the standard surgical technique is ulna plate and screw fixation (dynamic compression plate or locking plate), which allows restoration of the length, alignment and rotation of the ulna.
  • The plate is placed on the posterior or posterolateral aspect of the proximal ulna, with al at least 3 bicortical screws proximal and 3 distal to the fracture.
  • After fixation of the ulna, the radial head usually reduces spontaneously; If not, open reduction is performed and joint stability is evaluated.
  • Trap: do not fix the radius unless it is type IV; in types I-III, fix only the ulna; The plate must be long enough to avoid material failure in metaphyseal fractures.

items.foco: Radial head reduction after ulna fixation
items.title: Reduction sequence
items.contentHtml:
Reduction of the radial head after fixation of the ulna

  • The correct surgical sequence in the Monteggia lesion is: first fixation of the ulna with a plate and screws, and then verify the reduction of the radial head; this is usually achieved spontaneously by restoring the length of the ulna.
  • If the radial head is not reduced after fixation of the ulna, open reduction of the radial head should be performed and evaluated for the presence of soft tissue interposition (capsule, annular ligament) or fracture of the radial neck.
  • In children, closed reduction of the radial head is performed before immobilization; If this fails, open reduction and repair of the annular ligament are performed.
  • Trap: never attempt to reduce the radial head before fixing the ulna, since ulnar instability will prevent the reduction from being maintained.

items.foco: Not to be confused with Galeazzi fracture (distal radius)
items.title: Differential diagnosis with Galeazzi
items.contentHtml:
Not to be confused with Galeazzi fracture (distal radius)

  • Galeazzi fracture: fracture of the shaft of the radius (middle or distal third) with dislocation of the distal radioulnar joint (does not affect the radial head).
  • Monteggia injury: fracture of the proximal ulna with dislocation of the radial head (proximal joint).
  • Both are forearm injuries with double bone and joint injury, but the location of the dislocation is opposite: proximal in Monteggia, distal in Galeazzi.
  • Trap: on the radiograph, the radiocapitellar line is normal in Galeazzi (radial head aligned with humeral condyle), while in Monteggia it is displaced. Also not to be confused with Essex-Lopresti fracture (radial head fracture with distal radioulnar dislocation).

items.title: Injury mechanism
items.contentHtml:
Suspect in falls with a pronated forearm

  • The classic mechanism of Monteggia injury is a fall with the forearm in forced pronation, generating an axial load that fractures the ulna and dislocates the radial head anteriorly (type I).
  • In adults, the type II mechanism (flexion) occurs with the forearm in supination and the elbow in flexion, but the initial suspicion should include any fall with impact on the forearm.
  • In children, type III (lateral) injury occurs due to a forced varus mechanism of the elbow, often after a fall with the hand supported and the forearm in pronation.
  • Trap: in patients with isolated ulna fracture (cane stop fracture), always rule out radial head dislocation with an x-ray that includes the elbow; Do not assume that it is a simple fracture.
